summary: Gruber argues against TrackBack, Movable Type's notification protocol for linking between blogs, on the grounds that it is unnecessarily complex and exclusionary. He contends that TrackBack's requirement for both parties to run compatible software creates an unfair barrier that leaves out most of the web, including static sites, Blogger-powered blogs, and desktop publishing tools like Radio UserLand. Instead, he advocates for simpler alternatives: HTTP referrer tracking (which he has just implemented on Daring Fireball) and Technorati's link-indexing service, both of which work with plain HTML links and require no special software. He acknowledges TrackBack's richer data but argues that for communication protocols, lowest-common-denominator universality is a virtue, not a weakness.
key_points: TrackBack is a two-way protocol that only works between sites running compatible software, excluding the vast majority of the web from participation.
Simple HTTP referrer tracking and Technorati's link cosmos provide similar functionality to TrackBack without requiring any special software or protocols from either party.
For communication protocols and web standards, universal accessibility (lowest-common-denominator) is a feature, not a bug — unlike desktop software where differentiation is valuable.
TrackBack has practical usability problems: it's painful to send, relies on cryptic RDF for auto-discovery, and conflicts with static site generation.
The web's fundamental strength is that creating and linking between sites is simple; TrackBack undermines this by raising the barrier to participation.
money_quotes: A list of TrackBack pings on your site is only going to reflect incoming links from other web sites running TrackBack software. But most web sites don't speak TrackBack.
No dispute, it's useful and interesting to see a list of web sites linking to a particular article or web site. But it makes no sense to limit such a list to a small subset of the web.
The entire reason the web took off so quickly, and has continued to grow, is that it's really quite easy to create your own web site.
TrackBack is unnecessary as a connector between web sites. We already have something to connect web sites: the link.
The key to understanding why Technorati is so damn cool is that all you need to do to get tracked is link.
All you need to do is link. Technorati does the rest.
For communication protocols and web standards, lowest-common-denominator status is generally a good thing. The opposite is true for desktop software and hardware.
If you decide to display TrackBacks on your web site instead of referrers, you are excluding all of the people who aren't able or willing to use TrackBack for their web sites.
key_insight: The link is the web's native connection protocol, and any system that requires special software on both ends to track connections between sites is solving the wrong problem at the cost of the web's fundamental openness.
